stories.headline: Three wildland firefighters killed in western Colorado burnover identified as Rifle Helitack crew members
stories.summary:
Three federal firefighters killed Saturday while battling the Snyder Mesa fire near the Colorado-Utah border have been identified by the U.S. Forest Service as Emily Barker, 38, Nick Hutcherson, and a third crew member, all members of the Rifle Helitack interagency crew based in Garfield County. The five firefighters were overtaken by flames in a burnover event while fighting the Knowles fire in western Colorado and attempted to deploy fire shelters, but three did not survive. Two others sustained burn injuries and remained hospitalized in the Denver area in stable condition as of Monday.

Helitack crews use helicopters to insert firefighters into remote or difficult terrain. U.S. Wildland Fire Service Chief Brian Fennessy said at a Monday news conference that he was confident the crew had made sound decisions before the fire overtook them. 'The fires in this region over the decades, you know, killed many firefighters. They weren't being foolish. They weren't being careless,' Fennessy said.

The Snyder Mesa fire formed when two Utah fires merged Saturday and consumed the already-burning Knowles and Gore fires in Mesa County, growing to more than 30,000 acres as of Monday. Gov. Jared Polis had previously issued a disaster declaration for the fire and deployed the National Guard to assist firefighting efforts across the state.

The deaths have renewed attention to the limits of fire shelters used as last-resort protection by wildland firefighters. The current M-2002 shelter, introduced in 2004 after 14 firefighters died in the 1994 South Canyon fire near Glenwood Springs, offers improved protection but can still fail in flame temperatures exceeding 2,000 degrees Fahrenheit, as seen in the 2013 Yarnell Hill fire in Arizona.

stories.headline: Aspen Acres fire near Pueblo explodes to 23,000 acres, forces mandatory evacuations of Beulah, Rye and surrounding communities
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire, first reported before 6 a.m. Monday in Custer County near the Pueblo County line, grew from roughly 35 acres in the early morning to more than 23,000 acres by Monday evening, driven by winds estimated at 100 mph that grounded all aerial firefighting support. Mandatory evacuations were ordered for the communities of Beulah, Rye, San Isabel Lake and the Bishops Castle area, along with portions of Colorado 165 south to the Pueblo County line.

An unspecified number of structures were reported affected by the fire, according to the Pueblo County Sheriff's Office. Power outages hit more than 1,500 customers served by the San Isabel Electric Association, with the utility warning that lineworkers could not access equipment due to fire activity. Evacuation shelters were established at the Pueblo County Recreation Center and the Wetmore Community Center, with large animals directed to the Colorado State Fairgrounds.

Gov. Jared Polis issued a verbal disaster declaration for the Aspen Acres fire Monday, as he did for the Willow fire in Lake County, unlocking additional state resources including engine and hand crews and aerial assets. Pueblo County Sheriff David Lucero and Fire Chief Barb Huber placed the county under Stage 2 fire restrictions prohibiting the sale and use of fireworks.

The Pueblo Department of Public Health issued an air quality alert urging all county residents to remain indoors. The fire is among several major blazes burning simultaneously across Colorado, compounding the strain on statewide emergency response resources.

stories.headline: Multiple wildfires scorch more than 75,000 acres across Colorado; Gold Mountain, Ferris and Snyder fires remain uncontained
stories.summary:
Several major wildfires continued burning across Colorado on Monday with no containment on the largest blazes, according to the Colorado Sun wildfire tracker. The Gold Mountain fire northwest of Ouray reached 7,103 acres on private land, prompting a disaster emergency declaration from Gov. Polis. The Ferris fire in Montezuma and Dolores counties, formed from the merger of the Far Draw, Doe Canyon and Ferris fires, consumed more than 20,500 acres with high winds causing extreme fire behavior.

The Snyder Mesa fire near the Colorado-Utah border, where three federal firefighters died Saturday, stood at more than 30,000 acres and remained 0 percent contained. The Red Rock fire near DeBeque was the lone significant fire showing progress, reaching 60 percent containment at 340 acres after starting on BLM land northeast of Grand Junction.

Fire agencies from the Denver metro area deployed personnel and equipment to western Colorado to assist. Governor Polis said resources from other states, including crews from Alaska, were being brought in, and that Colorado had become a national priority for firefighting resources. More than 100 people were evacuated from the Colorado River area Saturday night in what Mesa County Search and Rescue described as the most operationally complex evacuation in living memory.

Smoke from the western Colorado fires reached the Front Range, prompting an air quality alert from the National Weather Service in Boulder for multiple pollutants including wildfire smoke. Denver and surrounding communities faced unhealthy ozone levels in addition to particulate matter from wildfire smoke.

stories.headline: Colorado holds primary election Tuesday for governor, U.S. Senate, eight congressional seats and dozens of legislative races
stories.summary:
Colorado voters cast ballots Tuesday in primary elections covering the governorship, a U.S. Senate seat, all eight U.S. House districts and dozens of state legislative seats. The highest-profile contest is the Democratic gubernatorial primary between U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet and state Attorney General Phil Weiser, both prominent statewide figures who would face Republican opposition in November in an increasingly blue state.

The race carries particular national significance because Bennet's decision to seek the governorship creates a potential U.S. Senate vacancy. If Bennet wins and takes office as governor, he would vacate his Senate seat, allowing the governor to appoint a replacement until a 2028 election. Bennet has said he would hold his seat until inauguration and name his own successor, indicating a preference for a Democrat under 50 — a list that includes Reps. Jason Crow, Joe Neguse and Brittany Pettersen, all of whom have endorsed him.

Polls close at 7 p.m. Tuesday. Colorado allows same-day voter registration and unaffiliated voters — more than half the electorate — may participate by returning one of the two partisan ballots they received. Ballots must be dropped at a drop box or in-person voting center by 7 p.m., as it is too late to mail them back.

Republican voters are also selecting a gubernatorial nominee. Results will be posted in real time by county clerk websites and the Colorado Secretary of State's office following poll closing.

stories.headline: Colorado Supreme Court unanimously blocks Democratic redistricting ballot measures, ending state's off-cycle congressional map fight
stories.summary:
The Colorado Supreme Court on Monday struck down three ballot measures that would have redrawn the state's congressional map to favor Democrats, removing Colorado from the nationwide off-cycle redistricting battle. The initiatives, funded by a group linked to U.S. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, would have bypassed the state's independent redistricting commission — created by voters in 2018 — to put new maps in place for elections in 2028 and 2030. Under the proposed maps, Democrats were projected to win seven of Colorado's eight congressional seats, up from four currently held.

In two unanimous decisions, the court ruled the proposals violated the state constitution's single-subject requirement, which mandates that ballot initiatives address only one central purpose. Chief Justice Monica Marquez wrote that temporarily altering the constitutionally mandated redistricting frequency 'represents a seismic shift to Colorado's longstanding redistricting process enshrined in the state constitution.'

Initiatives 241 and 242 were a novel attempt to split the plan into two linked measures to circumvent the single-subject rule, but the court rejected that approach as well in a second unanimous opinion authored by Justice Richard Gabriel. The decisions deliver a significant victory to Republicans, who have benefited more broadly from the national off-cycle redistricting push.

Because Colorado voters transferred redistricting power from the legislature to an independent commission in 2018, Democratic legislators had no direct path to redraw maps and needed voter approval to do so. The court's ruling forecloses that avenue entirely ahead of the November election.

stories.headline: Colorado positioned as nation's top quantum computing hub after Trump executive orders, Elevate Quantum CEO says
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump's recent signing of quantum computing executive orders has positioned Colorado and the Denver metro area as the strongest ecosystem in the nation to lead quantum technology development, according to Elevate Quantum CEO Jessi Olsen. The region is home to major federal labs, universities and private companies working in the sector, which industry leaders say gives Colorado a structural advantage in capturing federal investment and research contracts flowing from the new policy directives.

Separately, Denver-based energy company Liberty Energy announced it will pay $332.6 million for Wärtsilä power equipment as it expands its data center and industrial energy infrastructure business. The purchase underscores the growing intersection of energy infrastructure and technology investment in the Denver metro economy.

The Front Range Passenger Rail District is also exploring special event stops at Denver sports stadiums for its planned Coco Connector commuter rail line, a move that could attract approximately $300 million in additional investment to the project. Vantage Data Centers, another Denver-area firm, promoted Michael Stoner to chief development officer as the company scales artificial intelligence infrastructure projects across North America.

Taken together, the developments reflect a broader surge of technology and infrastructure investment centered on the Denver metro area, driven by both federal policy and private capital deployment.

stories.headline: Mayor Johnston partners with SpotHero to offer $5 downtown Denver parking through September
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston has announced a summer partnership with Chicago-based parking app SpotHero that will offer roughly 3,000 downtown parking spots for a flat $5 fee during designated hours through the end of September. The program, called 'Mayor's Park $5,' is intended to draw more visitors to downtown Denver as the city continues efforts to revitalize foot traffic and economic activity in the urban core.

The city is not spending public funds on the discounts; instead, SpotHero is absorbing the price reduction as part of the partnership. The reduced-price spots are available in the central downtown area bounded by Broadway and Wazee Street from east to west and 14th to 20th streets from south to north. Drivers can access the spots through SpotHero's smartphone app or web platform, with an additional 99-cent service fee waived using the promo code 'MILEHIGH5.'

'This program is a win for everyone,' Johnston said in a news release. 'It makes parking easier to find and afford, it encourages people to enjoy downtown, and it fills up spaces that might otherwise sit empty.' SpotHero typically lists Denver-area parking at $10 to $15.

The initiative comes as downtown Denver continues to recover from pandemic-era declines in office occupancy and retail activity. City officials have been pursuing a range of strategies to bring residents and visitors back to the central business district.

stories.headline: Arvada neighborhood plagued by repeated power outages; Xcel Energy says overly sensitive settings were to blame
stories.summary:
Residents and businesses in the Maple Valley neighborhood of west Arvada endured at least five power outages over two weeks, disrupting daily life and costing some businesses thousands of dollars in lost revenue. The affected area is generally bounded by 72nd and 64th avenues and Indiana and Eldridge streets, where customers of Xcel Energy reported outages occurring during daytime and weekend hours without advance notice from the utility.

Xcel Energy spokeswoman Lisa Andersen attributed the outages to overly sensitive safety settings on power lines near 64th Avenue and Indiana Street. The technology is designed to cut wildfire risk by rapidly stopping electricity flow if an object contacts a line, but the settings were being triggered by increased electrical demand during summer months. Crews corrected the settings last week, Andersen said.

Mile High Wine Cellars in the nearby Westwoods Shopping Center estimated losing $1,000 to $2,000 in business on each day an outage occurred. Employee Nick DiGiacomo said the shop closed early during outages because it could not process any transactions.

The incidents come amid broader scrutiny of Xcel Energy's outage management practices. The Colorado Public Utilities Commission is currently considering new rules governing proactive power shutoffs after a series of planned cutoffs last December left tens of thousands of Denver metro area and foothills customers without electricity for days.

stories.headline: Locksmith suspected of targeting older Denver metro adults faces theft charges
stories.summary:
A locksmith operating in the Denver metro area is now facing theft charges after investigators concluded the individual had been targeting older adults, according to reporting aggregated by the Denver Post. Specific details of the charges, the suspect's identity and the number of alleged victims were not immediately available in initial reports.

Authorities have not released the full scope of the investigation publicly, but the case underscores a recurring pattern of service-industry fraud targeting elderly residents in the region. Law enforcement agencies have previously warned consumers to verify credentials and get written estimates before allowing tradespeople into their homes.

The case is under active investigation. Additional charges or suspects have not been ruled out. Residents are advised to contact the Better Business Bureau or local law enforcement if they believe they have been victimized by fraudulent locksmith services.

stories.headline: Avalanche trade Valeri Nichushkin; Cale Makar eligible for contract extension July 1
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche have traded winger Valeri Nichushkin, according to reports aggregated Monday, continuing a busy offseason of roster moves for the club. Details of the trade, including the destination team and return package, were not fully detailed in initial reports. The move follows a string of recent transactions including trades involving Jack Drury, Fabian Lysell, Svechkov and L'Heureux, as well as new contracts signed by defensemen Brent Burns and Brett Kulak.

Separately, Avalanche superstar defenseman Cale Makar becomes eligible to sign a contract extension on July 1, one year before his current deal expires, a development that NHL insiders are closely watching. Makar is widely considered one of the best defensemen in the league, and any extension negotiation is expected to draw significant national attention.

The Avalanche also released their 2026 Development Camp roster Monday, featuring 27 players split among 11 forwards, 12 defensemen and four goaltenders. Development camp provides younger prospects and recent draft picks an opportunity to work with coaching staff ahead of training camp.

stories.headline: Rockies drop two of three to Twins in Minnesota, fall to 33-52 on the season
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies lost 3-2 at Minnesota on Sunday, dropping two of three games to the Twins in the series and remaining among the worst records in the National League. The Rockies stand at 33-52 overall and 18-23 at home entering a Monday home game against the Miami Marlins at Coors Field.

Despite the series loss, Colorado showed flashes of offensive life, including a seven-run rally that briefly gave the Rockies a lead in Friday night's 9-8 extra-inning loss. Right-hander Tanner Gordon, recovering from a right hip impingement, had a strong rehab start with Triple-A Albuquerque last week and was in the Rockies' clubhouse ahead of the Marlins series, with a start expected. Right-hander Tomoyuki Sugano is dealing with a finger issue and is expected to be pushed back to the upcoming series against the San Francisco Giants.

stories.headline: Nuggets face active offseason with Aaron Gordon drawing trade interest as free agency opens July 1
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets are entering a busy stretch of roster decisions as NBA free agency opens July 1, with the front office signaling it is willing to discuss trades for any player except three-time MVP Nikola Jokic. Aaron Gordon has drawn the most trade interest among non-Jokic starters, though the Nuggets are said to prefer keeping Gordon and Jamal Murray while exploring deals for Cameron Johnson or Christian Braun, according to NBA insiders Marc Stein and Jake Fischer.

Center Jonas Valanciunas is not expected to return next season, and the same may apply to guard Tim Hardaway Jr., per the same reporting. Tennessee State's Aaron Nkrumah signed an Exhibit-10 contract with the Nuggets following the draft, while Howard University's Bryce Harris joined the Oklahoma City Thunder for Summer League.

The Nuggets also have coaching staff adjustments under consideration, which could create uncertainty around assistant coaches including David Adelman, according to analysis from Nugg Love. The team traded down at last week's NBA Draft and selected Trevon Brazile at No. 35, a decision that drew scrutiny from local columnists questioning the front office's draft strategy.

weather.notable: Nearly 9 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Boy, those French, they have a different word for everything.

topStory.headline: Supreme Court expands presidential firing power, blocks Trump on Federal Reserve in split rulings
topStory.summary:
The U.S. Supreme Court issued four significant rulings Monday affecting President Donald Trump's executive authority, with the justices ruling in his favor on one major case and against him on three others. The decisions touched on presidential power over independent agencies, the independence of the Federal Reserve, mail-in ballot counting deadlines, and a civil sexual assault verdict.

In the most consequential ruling, a 6-3 conservative majority overturned a 90-year-old precedent in Trump v. Slaughter, holding that removal protections for members of independent federal agencies are unconstitutional. Chief Justice John Roberts, writing for the majority, held that officials who exercise executive power must be subject to removal by the president. The case centered on Trump's 2025 firing of Federal Trade Commission member Rebecca Slaughter without cause. In a rare bench dissent, Justice Sonia Sotomayor warned the ruling would transform dozens of independent commissions into purely executive agencies, saying 'chaos will follow.'

In a separate 5-4 decision, the court blocked Trump from immediately removing Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook, preserving the central bank's independence from direct presidential interference. Trump had sought to oust Cook citing unproven mortgage fraud allegations, but the court ruled she may remain in her post while litigation proceeds. No president had previously attempted to remove a sitting Fed governor since the central bank was established in 1913.

The court also declined to review a New York jury's 2024 verdict finding Trump civilly liable for sexually abusing writer E. Jean Carroll and subsequently defaming her, offering no explanation or noted dissents. In a fourth ruling, the justices upheld laws in more than a dozen states that allow mail-in ballots arriving after Election Day to be counted, rejecting a challenge backed by national Republicans and the Trump administration.

The FTC ruling is expected to have wide-ranging implications for more than two dozen multi-member independent agencies, including the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, the Nuclear Regulatory Commission and the National Labor Relations Board, all of which have historically been shielded from at-will presidential removal. Trump celebrated the FTC decision on social media, calling it one of the most important rulings ever issued regarding presidential powers. Critics, including Democratic Sen. Elizabeth Warren, argued the decision would allow Trump to install political allies at agencies designed to operate free from partisan control.
